title: 08 — Integrate deterministic package-lock repair into pull-request validation
objective: Integrate canonical package-lock regeneration into the pull-request CI/CD lifecycle so eligible same-repository PR branches receive a deterministic npm-generated package-lock.json before dependency-dependent validation proceeds.
userContext: The repository has a manual package-lock repair workflow that uses the canonical Node.js and npm versions, validates the regenerated dependency graph, discards validation side effects, and commits only package-lock.json. Maintainers want this behaviour integrated into eligible pull requests so stale or manually edited lockfiles are repaired on the PR branch before normal tests, linting, type checking, application builds, Worker builds, and deployment checks run.
currentBehaviour: Package-lock repair is available as a manual recovery workflow. Pull requests that change package metadata can enter normal validation with an inconsistent lockfile, fail npm ci, or require a maintainer to run the repair workflow and update the branch manually.
requiredBehaviour: Eligible trusted same-repository pull requests must receive a canonical package-lock.json repair before substantive dependency-dependent validation. The repair must update only the PR branch, commit only package-lock.json, trigger final validation against the repaired head commit, remain idempotent and loop-safe, and preserve the repository security and branch-protection model.
userExperience: Contributors and maintainers receive an automatically repaired lockfile on eligible PR branches, followed by normal CI validation against the repaired commit. Failures clearly distinguish lockfile generation, validation, permission, branch-update, and final-verification problems.
functionalRequirements: Define deterministic classification for pull requests requiring package-lock repair.
At minimum, consider changes to package.json, package-lock.json, .nvmrc, .node-version, package-manager declarations, and dependency-management configuration that affects lockfile generation.
Reuse or extend the existing repository change-classification architecture rather than creating duplicate file-classification logic.
Run automatic write-capable repair only for eligible same-repository PR branches that satisfy the trusted security model.
Do not run privileged repair against fork pull requests, arbitrary commits, tags, or untrusted external branches.
Document and safely handle Dependabot and other bot PRs according to available token and branch permissions.
Reuse the existing deterministic package-lock regeneration implementation rather than duplicating substantial shell logic between manual and PR workflows.
Prefer a reusable workflow, repository script, or other single implementation shared by manual recovery and automatic PR repair.
Use Node.js from .nvmrc and the exact npm version from package.json#packageManager.
Regenerate package-lock.json using the canonical npm version.
Save the regenerated lockfile outside the repository working tree before validation.
Verify the regenerated dependency graph through a clean npm ci.
Discard all tracked and untracked changes produced by validation or framework tooling.
Restore only the saved regenerated package-lock.json after validation.
Verify both the working-tree diff and staged diff contain exactly package-lock.json before committing.
Do not maintain an allow-list of framework-generated files such as next-env.d.ts or tsconfig.json.
Do not create a commit when the regenerated lockfile is identical to the PR branch version.
Commit the canonical lockfile directly to the eligible PR branch using a clear automation commit message.
Use PR head-SHA validation immediately before publishing so an older repair job cannot overwrite newer contributor changes.
Use safe lease-based push protection and stop when the remote branch has advanced unexpectedly.
Sequence the PR lifecycle so dependency-dependent validation runs against the repaired PR head commit rather than the original pre-repair commit.
Ensure required checks and trusted auto-merge cannot approve or merge the pre-repair commit when a repair commit has been added.
Prevent repair commits from causing infinite workflow recursion.
Recognise automation-generated lockfile repair commits and avoid regenerating or recommitting an identical lockfile.
Use PR-scoped concurrency so stale repair or validation jobs cannot overwrite or authorise newer work.
Preserve the manual package-lock repair workflow as an operational recovery mechanism.
Provide an actionable fallback when automatic branch updates are not permitted, such as a workflow artifact or precise manual-repair instruction.
Write a clear GitHub Actions summary distinguishing classification, generation, clean installation, validation, commit, push, skipped repair, and final verification outcomes.
Update documentation and specs/000-current-application-spec.md to describe the automatic repair stage, security boundaries, two-commit PR lifecycle, fallback behaviour, and manual recovery path.
technicalConsiderations: The intended lifecycle is contributor or bot commit, trusted lockfile repair commit where needed, and final required validation against the repaired PR head SHA.

Classification and metadata inspection may run before repair, but npm ci, tests, linting, type checking, application builds, Worker builds, and deployment validation must ultimately run against the repaired head commit.
Do not continue a validation job using an old checkout after the workflow has pushed a repair commit.
Do not use pull_request_target to check out or execute untrusted PR code.
Separate unprivileged classification and metadata work from privileged branch mutation.
Grant contents: write only to the trusted repair job and pull-requests: read only where PR metadata or head verification requires it.
Do not bypass branch protection, use an administrator override, or push directly to protected main.
Validate user-controlled branch and ref values before using them in shell commands or Git operations.
Keep GitHub Action references pinned to immutable full commit SHAs with accurate adjacent version comments.
Use bounded retries for transient GitHub API operations where applicable and re-query state after ambiguous failures to preserve idempotency.
A successful lockfile commit and branch push must not be reported as a lockfile-generation failure merely because a later metadata operation times out.
The design must remain compatible with required status checks, trusted auto-merge, sensitive-file classification, Dependabot handling, and the existing reusable workflow architecture.
outOfScope: Automatically changing dependency declarations or choosing dependency versions.
Resolving incompatible dependency selections made by contributors or Dependabot.
Weakening or skipping normal pull-request checks.
Bypassing repository rules or branch protection.
Committing validation-generated application, framework, TypeScript, or build files.
Removing the manual package-lock repair recovery workflow.
Running privileged write-capable automation against untrusted fork code.
acceptanceCriteria: Eligible same-repository pull requests with lockfile-relevant changes automatically receive a canonical package-lock.json before substantive dependency-dependent validation.
Pull requests without lockfile-relevant changes skip repair without unnecessary writes or workflow noise.
Fork and untrusted pull requests cannot trigger privileged checkout, execution, or branch mutation.
Only package-lock.json can be committed by the automatic repair stage.
All validation-generated tracked and untracked changes are discarded without maintaining a generated-file allow-list.
No-change regeneration completes successfully without creating a commit.
A stale repair job cannot overwrite a newer contributor or bot commit.
Repair commits are idempotent and do not create infinite workflow loops.
Final required validation runs against the repaired PR head SHA.
Required checks and trusted auto-merge cannot approve the obsolete pre-repair SHA.
The canonical Node.js and npm versions are used for lockfile generation.
A clean npm ci validates the regenerated lockfile.
The manual repair workflow remains available and shares the canonical repair implementation.
Dependabot and other bot PR behaviour follows a documented secure policy.
Permission or branch-update limitations produce an actionable fallback.
Workflow summaries clearly report classification, generation, validation, commit, push, fallback, and final verification outcomes.

Repository workflow and security tests cover repair eligibility, fork protection, stale-head protection, single-file commit enforcement, cleanup of validation side effects, no-change behaviour, loop prevention, final-head validation, permission boundaries, and immutable action pins.
Workflow YAML parses successfully and actionlint passes where available.
npm run toolchain:validate, npm test, npm run lint, npm run typecheck, npm run issue:validate, relevant build checks, and git diff --check pass.
Documentation and specs/000-current-application-spec.md describe the automatic PR lockfile repair lifecycle and security model.
codexGuidance: Implement after DEV-006 and independently of DEV-007. Reuse the existing deterministic manual package-lock repair logic and integrate it into the orchestrated PR lifecycle rather than duplicating it. Explicitly design and test the two-commit lifecycle so final required checks and auto-merge operate only on the repaired PR head SHA.
